_description: Methodology template: Natural Hazard Exposure Analysis. Pre-fills analysis section with evidence-based method stack for composite hazard × vulnerability exposure work at the census-tract level.

client.notes: Confirm which hazard types are in scope (flood, wildfire, wind, earthquake, heat). Each requires different data sources. This template covers flood + wildfire as primary hazards.

audience.role: Emergency manager, resilience planner, or climate adaptation officer
audience.what_they_are_deciding: Where to invest in hazard mitigation, which communities need evacuation planning support, and how to prioritize BRIC or HMGP grant applications.
audience.what_they_already_believe: Low-income and minority communities are more exposed to natural hazards and less able to recover.
audience.political_or_institutional_context: FEMA Equity in Resilience framework emphasizes SVI. Use FEMA language for grant-facing work. Avoid climate change framing if politically sensitive in jurisdiction.

engagement.hero_question: Which census tracts face the highest compound exposure to natural hazards, accounting for both the physical hazard and the social vulnerability of resident populations?
sensitive_findings_to_handle_carefully: FEMA flood zone designations affect property insurance — naming specific parcels/neighborhoods can cause alarm
Wildfire risk maps are politically contested in some Western states
SVI race/ethnicity components may be sensitive — frame as vulnerability, not as demographic targeting

known_spatial_issues: FEMA NFHL flood zones are at parcel/structure level — aggregation to tract requires zonal statistics
Wildfire risk rasters (USFS FSIM, First Street) are at 30m or 1km resolution — align projection before zonal stats
SVI is available at tract and county level — confirm which resolution client needs
Tract centroids may fall in water or non-residential areas — use area-weighted overlay, not centroid

primary_sources.name: FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL)
primary_sources.notes: Download DFIRM geodatabase for study area. Extract S_FLD_HAZ_AR layer. Reclassify: Zone A/AE/AH/AO/VE = high risk (1), Zone X-shaded = moderate (0.5), Zone X-unshaded = minimal (0). US-only.

primary_sources.name: USFS Wildfire Hazard Potential (WHP)
primary_sources.notes: 30m raster. Reclassify to 1-5 scale. Run zonal_stats.py to aggregate mean WHP per tract. Continental US only — Alaska/Hawaii use different products.

primary_sources.name: First Street Foundation Flood/Fire Risk (alternative)
primary_sources.notes: Higher resolution than FEMA/USFS. Free for academic/nonprofit use. Check license for commercial client work.

primary_sources.name: CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (SVI)
primary_sources.notes: Download tract-level SVI for study state(s). Use RPL_THEMES (overall percentile rank) as socially_vulnerable_population column. Four theme components available for disaggregation.

primary_sources.name: ACS 5-Year Estimates
primary_sources.notes: For population denominator and to supplement SVI with custom vulnerability metrics if needed.

primary_sources.name: TIGER/Line Tracts
primary_sources.notes: Use vintage matching ACS/SVI data year

known_data_quality_issues: FEMA NFHL coverage is incomplete in some counties — check for unmapped areas before analysis
USFS WHP does not cover areas east of the Great Plains — use FEMA NFHL flood risk as primary hazard for Eastern US
SVI is recalculated each year — rankings shift even if underlying conditions are stable
Zonal statistics of rasters to tract polygons assumes uniform within-tract distribution — flag large tracts
institutional_areas_to_flag: Military bases, airports, and large industrial areas may dominate tract-level hazard scores
Uninhabited or sparsely populated high-hazard tracts skew exposure statistics — weight by population

analysis.dependent_variable_notes: Composite: exposure_index = hazard_score × svi_percentile. hazard_score = weighted mean of flood_zone_pct_area and wildfire_risk_mean (normalize each 0-1 before combining). Weight by client priority — equal weights by default.

analysis_notes.bivariate_choropleth: Use analyze_bivariate.py with hazard_score and svi_rpl_themes. 3×3 or 2×2 grid. Top-right cell (high hazard + high vulnerability) is the policy priority. Label this cell explicitly in the legend.
analysis_notes.zonal_stats: Run zonal_stats.py for both rasters. For flood zones: compute pct_area of each hazard zone per tract (intersect NFHL polygons with tracts, compute area fractions). For wildfire: mean WHP value per tract.
analysis_notes.proportional_symbols: Size circles by exposed_population (population in tracts above exposure_index threshold). Overlay on choropleth of exposure_index. Tells the story of where people actually live in high-hazard areas.
analysis_notes.hotspots: Gi* on exposure_index. Identify significant clusters of compound exposure. These are the BRIC/HMGP priority areas.
analysis_notes.exposure_threshold: Define exposure_index threshold for 'high exposure' in consultation with client. Common choices: top quintile (80th percentile), or SVI > 0.75 AND hazard_score > 0.5.

report.pyramid_lead: [N] census tracts in [Study Area] face high combined natural hazard exposure and social vulnerability, placing an estimated [X] residents at elevated risk with limited capacity to prepare, respond, and recover.

scqa.situation: [Study Area] spans [N] census tracts across [geography]. The region faces [flood/wildfire/both] hazard risk, with [X]% of tracts in FEMA Special Flood Hazard Areas and [Y]% in high wildfire hazard potential zones.
scqa.complication: High-hazard areas are not evenly distributed across the population. [X] tracts combine above-average hazard scores with SVI rankings above the [Nth] percentile — meaning residents face outsized physical risk with fewer resources to adapt.
scqa.question: Which specific communities face the compound burden of high hazard exposure and high social vulnerability, and how many residents do they represent?
scqa.answer: The [N] highest-priority tracts — identified through a combined hazard × vulnerability index — are home to [X] residents and concentrated in [geography]. These tracts are the strongest candidates for BRIC/HMGP grant targeting and community resilience investment.

additional_checks: Verify NFHL coverage completeness — flag counties with no DFIRM data
Check WHP raster projection matches tract CRS before zonal stats
Validate SVI GEOID format matches TIGER tract GEOID (leading zeros)
Flag tracts with population < 200 — exposure statistics unreliable for very small populations

_methodology_caveats: FEMA is US-only: FEMA NFHL and SVI are United States federal products. For international study areas, substitute: UNDRR flood exposure data, JRC flood hazard maps, and INFORM Risk Index or WorldRiskIndex for vulnerability.

Modeled hazard zones are not guarantees: FEMA flood zones represent the 1% annual chance flood (100-year flood) modeled at the time of the last FIRM update — many FIRMs are 10-20 years old. Climate-adjusted flood risk (First Street) may differ substantially. Wildfire risk models use historical fire behavior, not projected future conditions.

Exposure index is a policy tool, not an actuarial product: The composite exposure_index is designed to rank and prioritize communities for planning purposes. It should not be used to set insurance rates or make individual property determinations.

Hazard weight assumptions: Equal weighting of flood and wildfire risk is a default assumption. For coastal counties, flood may dominate. For Western mountain communities, wildfire may be the primary hazard. Adjust weights in consultation with the client and document the rationale.

SVI and race correlation: SVI's 'Minority Status and Language' theme (Theme 3) explicitly includes race/ethnicity. The composite SVI score therefore partly reflects racial demographics. When presenting bivariate hazard × SVI maps, be precise: you are showing vulnerability, which correlates with but is not identical to race.

Population weighting matters: Raw exposure_index at the tract level can obscure that some high-scoring tracts are sparsely populated. Always pair choropleth maps with proportional symbols scaled by population to show where people actually live in harm's way.
